## Overview

The Application Insights adapter connects Agent Profiler directly to an Azure Application Insights workspace. It queries the `dependencies` table using KQL to retrieve spans collected by the [OTel Gateway](/agent-profiler/tool/otel-gateway/) and converts them into `Session` objects.

## When to Use This Adapter

Use the Application Insights adapter when:

- You want to browse historical sessions collected from multiple developer machines.
- You do not have direct access to the local `.jsonl` log files.
- You need to query spans across a time range or by session ID.

For real-time local sessions, the [Copilot CLI adapter](/agent-profiler/tool/adapters/copilot-cli/) reading directly from log files is faster.

## Prerequisites

- An Azure Application Insights resource with spans ingested via the [OTel Gateway](/agent-profiler/tool/otel-gateway/).
- An Azure identity (user or managed identity) with the **Monitoring Reader** role on the Application Insights resource.

## Configuration

Connection details will be configured in **Settings → Data Sources → Application Insights**:

| Setting | Description |
|---|---|
| Subscription ID | Azure subscription containing the Application Insights resource |
| Resource Group | Resource group name |
| Application Insights Name | Resource name |
| Lookback window | How far back to query (e.g., 7 days) |

## Overview

The Copilot CLI adapter reads session transcript files produced by the GitHub Copilot CLI (`gh copilot`). These are newline-delimited JSON (`.jsonl`) files stored on the local filesystem.

## Log File Location

Copilot CLI session logs are typically stored at:

- **macOS / Linux**: `~/.config/github-copilot/sessions/`
- **Windows**: `%APPDATA%\GitHub Copilot\sessions\`

Each session is a single `.jsonl` file named by session ID.

## What Is Parsed

The adapter extracts:

- Turn boundaries and user messages
- Assistant responses with token counts (input, output, cache read, cache write)
- Tool call events (`execute_tool`, `permission`, `report_intent`)
- Model information (`claude-opus-4.6`, etc.)
- Session-level metrics (total tokens, duration, compaction events)

## Session vs OTel Traces

The Copilot CLI adapter parses **local log files** and does not require the [OTel Gateway](/agent-profiler/tool/otel-gateway/). For centralised, cross-machine visibility, combine both: use the CLI adapter for local sessions and the [Application Insights adapter](/agent-profiler/tool/adapters/application-insights/) for gateway-collected traces.

## Configuration

Point the adapter at your log directory in **Settings → Data Sources → Copilot CLI**:

| Setting | Description |
|---|---|
| Log directory | Path to the directory containing `.jsonl` session files |
| Watch for new files | Automatically reload when new sessions are detected |

Connecting AI agent frameworks and data sources to Agent Profiler via OpenTelemetry adapters.

Adapters are the ingestion layer of Agent Profiler. Each adapter knows how to parse session data from a specific source — a log file format, an API, or a live telemetry stream — and converts it into the canonical `Session` domain model defined in `@agent-profiler/core`.

## Available Adapters

| Adapter | Source | Package |
|---|---|---|
| [Copilot CLI](/agent-profiler/tool/adapters/copilot-cli/) | GitHub Copilot CLI `.jsonl` logs | `@agent-profiler/adapters-copilot-cli` |
| [Application Insights](/agent-profiler/tool/adapters/application-insights/) | Azure Application Insights via KQL | `@agent-profiler/adapters-app-insights` |
| [Custom Adapters](/agent-profiler/tool/adapters/custom-adapters/) | Any source via the plugin contract | See guide |

## How Adapters Work

Every adapter implements the `SessionDataSource` interface from `@agent-profiler/core`:

```typescript
interface SessionDataSource {
  listSessions(): Promise<SessionSummary[]>;
  getSession(id: string): Promise<Session>;
}
```

The desktop app calls `listSessions()` to populate the session browser and `getSession()` when a session is opened. The adapter handles all source-specific I/O and parsing internally.

## Parser Conventions

- Adapters never throw on parse errors — they set `parseStatus: 'partial'` on the returned `Session` instead.
- Token counts unavailable in a source are set to `0`.
- The golden fixture in `__tests__/fixtures/` defines the expected output shape for each adapter.

## Architecture Decision

See [ADR-0004](/agent-profiler/project/decisions/adr-0004-vscode-adapter-split/) for the rationale behind splitting VS Code Copilot Chat and VS Code coding-agent into separate adapter packages.

Agent Profiler supports multiple data source types. You can configure one or more sources, and sessions from all sources appear together in the [Session Browser](/agent-profiler/tool/desktop/session-browser/).

## Supported Data Sources

| Source | Description | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Local file system | Read Copilot CLI `.jsonl` logs from a directory | Planned |
| OTel Gateway | Connect to an OTLP collector endpoint | Planned |
| Application Insights | Query spans from an Azure Application Insights workspace | Planned |
| VS Code storage | Read VS Code Copilot transcripts | Planned |

## Adding a Data Source

Data sources are configured in **Settings → Data Sources**. See [Settings](/agent-profiler/tool/desktop/settings/) for the full configuration reference.

## OTel Gateway Connection

To connect to the OTel Gateway, you will need the collector FQDN and (for private environments) credentials. See the [OTel Gateway section](/agent-profiler/tool/otel-gateway/) for setup details.
Overview of the Agent Profiler desktop application — session timeline, span detail, filtering, and settings.

The Agent Profiler desktop application is an Electron app that ingests OpenTelemetry traces from AI agent sessions and renders them as interactive timeline views.

## Key Features

- **Session Browser** — list, search, and open all locally available agent sessions
- **Timeline View** — flame-graph style rendering of span hierarchies, colour-coded by span type
- **Span Detail Panel** — inspect attributes, events, and resource metadata for any selected span
- **Data Source Configuration** — connect to local log directories, OTel Gateway, or Application Insights
- **Settings** — configure appearance, data paths, and adapter preferences

## Overview

The Session Browser is the main landing view when Agent Profiler opens. It lists all agent sessions discovered from your configured data sources and lets you search, filter, and open them.

## Planned Features

- **Session list** — chronological view of all sessions with duration, model, and tool-call count
- **Search** — full-text search across session metadata and span names
- **Filters** — filter by date range, model, source adapter, or status
- **Quick preview** — hover over a session to see a summary timeline without opening it
- **Comparison mode** — select two sessions to open them side by side

## Opening a Session

Click any session in the list to open its full timeline view. Use the breadcrumb at the top of the timeline to return to the session browser.

## Overview

The Settings panel is accessible from the application menu or the gear icon in the toolbar. It is organised into the following sections:

## General

- **Theme** — Light, Dark, or System default
- **Language** — Interface language (English only in v1)
- **Updates** — Check for updates automatically

## Data Sources

Configure which directories and remote endpoints Agent Profiler reads sessions from. See [Data Sources](/agent-profiler/tool/desktop/data-sources/) for a full explanation of each source type.

## Adapters

Enable or disable specific adapter parsers. Disabling an adapter prevents it from parsing sessions from that source, which can improve performance if you do not use that source type.

## Advanced

- **Log level** — controls verbosity of the application log (Error, Warning, Info, Debug)
- **Cache location** — path where parsed session data is cached
- **Reset to defaults** — restores all settings to their factory values
Configure the Copilot CLI to send OpenTelemetry traces to the OTel Gateway using environment variables.

import { Steps } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

Configure your shell environment so that the Copilot CLI exports OTLP traces to the gateway on every session.

## Environment Variables

Two environment variables control telemetry export from the Copilot CLI:

| Variable | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `COPILOT_OTEL_ENABLED` | `true` | Enables OTLP trace export from the CLI |
| `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` | `https://<collector-fqdn>` | The full HTTPS URL of the OTel Collector |

### Demo environment endpoint

```
https://<your-collector-fqdn>
```

## Shell Setup (macOS / Linux)

Add the following lines to your `~/.zshrc` (Zsh) or `~/.bashrc` (Bash):

```bash
export COPILOT_OTEL_ENABLED=true
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=https://<your-collector-fqdn>
```

After editing the file, reload your shell:

```bash
source ~/.zshrc   # or source ~/.bashrc
```

:::tip
If you have access to a private `dev` or `prod` environment, replace the endpoint with the appropriate collector FQDN from the Pulumi stack outputs.
:::

## Verification

<Steps>
1. **Check that the collector is reachable**

   ```bash
   curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://<your-collector-fqdn>
   ```

   A response of `200` confirms the collector is healthy and accepting connections. A timeout or connection refusal may indicate a cold start — wait 30 seconds and retry.

2. **Start a Copilot CLI session**

   Open a new terminal (so the environment variables are loaded) and run a Copilot CLI command that invokes at least one tool, for example:

   ```bash
   gh copilot suggest "list all files in the current directory"
   ```

3. **Verify spans in Application Insights**

   In the Azure portal, navigate to the Application Insights resource and run:

   ```kusto
   dependencies
   | where timestamp > ago(15m)
   | project timestamp, name, duration, resultCode
   | order by timestamp desc
   | take 20
   ```

   You should see spans such as `chat claude-opus-4.6`, `execute_tool bash`, and `permission` appearing within a minute of your session.
</Steps>

## VS Code Copilot Chat

:::caution
**VS Code Copilot Chat does not currently support OTEL export.** The `COPILOT_OTEL_ENABLED` environment variable is only respected by the **Copilot CLI** (`gh copilot`). Traces from VS Code Copilot Chat sessions are not forwarded to the gateway.
:::

For VS Code session data, use the VS Code adapter instead. See [Adapters](/agent-profiler/tool/adapters/) for details.
Centralised telemetry collection for GitHub Copilot CLI sessions — receives OTLP signals and routes them to Azure Application Insights.

The OTel Gateway is a managed OpenTelemetry Collector deployment on Azure Container Apps that acts as the telemetry bridge between developer machines running the Copilot CLI and Azure Application Insights.

## Purpose

Every Copilot CLI session emits rich telemetry — tool invocations, model calls, permission checks, and latency data. The gateway collects these signals centrally so that teams can query and analyse agent behaviour across all sessions without requiring each developer to configure their own observability backend.

## Architecture

```
Copilot CLI (developer machine)
        │ OTLP/HTTP traces
        ▼
OTel Collector (Azure Container App)
        │ azuremonitor exporter
        ▼
Azure Application Insights
        │ KQL queries
        ▼
Azure Monitor / Workbooks
```

### Data flow

1. The Copilot CLI emits `OTLP/HTTP` traces to the collector's FQDN on port 443.
2. The collector receives signals via the `otlp` receiver.
3. The `azuremonitor` exporter forwards processed spans to Application Insights.
4. Spans land in the `dependencies` table in the Application Insights workspace.

## Signals Captured

The gateway captures **traces** (spans). Each span represents a discrete unit of work within a Copilot CLI session:

| Span name | Description |
|---|---|
| `chat claude-opus-4.6` | Top-level model invocation with the specified model |
| `execute_tool bash` | Shell command execution via the bash tool |
| `execute_tool report_intent` | Intent reporting tool call |
| `permission` | Permission check before a sensitive tool is invoked |

Metrics and logs are not currently collected.

## Environments

Three deployment environments exist, each with its own Pulumi stack and Azure resource group:

| Environment | Networking | Authentication | Replicas | Use case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **demo** | Public HTTPS (no auth) | None | 0–2 (scale to zero) | Shared demo access |
| **dev** | Private (VNet + Private Endpoints) | Key Vault | 1–5 | Development and testing |
| **prod** | Private (VNet + Private Endpoints) | Key Vault | 3–10 | Production workloads |

### Demo endpoint

The demo environment is publicly accessible at:

```
https://<your-collector-fqdn>
```

:::caution
The demo environment scales to zero when idle. The first request after a period of inactivity may take 10–30 seconds as the container starts. See [Troubleshooting](/agent-profiler/tool/otel-gateway/troubleshooting/) for details.
:::

Diagnose and resolve common OTel Gateway connectivity, cold-start, and deployment issues.

## Connectivity Issues

### Collector does not respond

1. Verify the FQDN resolves correctly:

   ```bash
   nslookup <your-collector-fqdn>
   ```

2. Test HTTP connectivity:

   ```bash
   curl -v https://<your-collector-fqdn>
   ```

   A healthy collector returns `HTTP 200`. Any other status code indicates a deployment or configuration issue.

3. Check that your network allows outbound HTTPS (port 443). Corporate proxies or VPNs may block direct connections to Azure Container Apps.

### Spans not appearing in Application Insights

- Confirm `COPILOT_OTEL_ENABLED=true` is set in your **current** shell session (not just in the file — reload with `source ~/.zshrc`).
- Allow 1–2 minutes for spans to propagate through the batch processor to Application Insights.
- Run the [KQL diagnostic query](#kql-diagnostic-queries) below with a wider time window.

## Cold Starts

The demo environment scales to zero when idle (`minReplicas: 0`). The first OTLP request after a period of inactivity triggers a cold start:

- **Expected delay**: 10–30 seconds for the container to initialise.
- **Symptom**: The CLI appears to hang briefly on the first tool call.
- **Resolution**: Wait for the cold start to complete. Subsequent requests within the same session are fast.

To avoid cold starts in production workloads, set `minReplicas: 1` or higher in the Pulumi stack configuration.

## KQL Diagnostic Queries

Use these queries in **Application Insights → Logs** to verify telemetry ingestion.

### Check recent spans

```kusto
dependencies
| where timestamp > ago(1h)
| project timestamp, name, duration, resultCode
| order by timestamp desc
```

### Count spans by name

```kusto
dependencies
| where timestamp > ago(24h)
| summarize count() by name
| order by count_ desc
```

### Find spans from a specific session

```kusto
dependencies
| where timestamp > ago(1h)
| where customDimensions.session_id == "<your-session-id>"
| project timestamp, name, duration, customDimensions
| order by timestamp asc
```

## Common Errors

### 403 Forbidden on Key Vault

**Symptom**: `Pulumi up` or Container App startup fails with a Key Vault 403 error.

**Cause**: Your identity does not have the required RBAC role on the Key Vault resource.

**Resolution**: Elevate your permissions via **Azure PIM (Privileged Identity Management)** and request the `Key Vault Secrets User` role on the relevant Key Vault resource. Allow 5–10 minutes for the role assignment to propagate.

---

### `WorkloadProfilePropertyNotSupportedInApiVersion`

**Symptom**: Pulumi deployment fails with this error when creating or updating the Container App Environment.

**Cause**: The default `azure-native` Pulumi provider version uses an API version that does not support the `workloadProfiles` property.

**Resolution**: Pin the Container App Environment resource to API version `2024-03-01`:

```typescript
import * as app from "@pulumi/azure-native/app/v20240301";
```

---

### `ManagedEnvironmentSubnetDelegationError`

**Symptom**: Container App Environment deployment fails when `enablePrivateNetworking: true`.

**Cause**: The subnet assigned to the Container App Environment has not been delegated to `Microsoft.App/environments`.

**Resolution**: Ensure the subnet delegation is configured in the Pulumi stack:

```typescript
delegations: [{
  name: "app-environments-delegation",
  serviceName: "Microsoft.App/environments",
}],
```

---

## Collector Health Check

The OTel Collector exposes a health check endpoint at the root path. A `GET /` request to the collector FQDN returns `HTTP 200` when the collector is running and ready to accept traces.

```bash
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
  https://<your-collector-fqdn>
# Expected: 200
```

If the health check fails, check Container App logs in the Azure portal under **Container App → Monitoring → Log stream**.
Pulumi TypeScript stacks, Azure resources, and deployment instructions for the OTel Gateway.

The OTel Gateway infrastructure is defined as code using **Pulumi with TypeScript**. All infrastructure lives in `infra/otel/` within the monorepo.

## Stack Management

Three Pulumi stacks correspond to the three deployment environments:

| Stack file | Environment | Networking |
|---|---|---|
| `Pulumi.demo.yaml` | demo | Public |
| `Pulumi.dev.yaml` | dev | Private (VNet) |
| `Pulumi.prod.yaml` | prod | Private (VNet) |

### Deploy a stack

```bash
cd infra/otel
pulumi up -s demo     # or dev, prod
```

### Preview changes without deploying

```bash
pulumi preview -s demo
```

### Destroy a stack

```bash
pulumi destroy -s demo
```

## Key Configuration Flags

Each stack's YAML file exposes the following configuration keys under `config:`:

| Key | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `enablePrivateNetworking` | `boolean` | When `true`, provisions a VNet, Private Endpoints, and Key Vault. Required for `dev` and `prod`. |
| `minReplicas` | `number` | Minimum Container App replicas. Set to `0` in demo to enable scale-to-zero. |
| `maxReplicas` | `number` | Maximum Container App replicas under load. |
| `concurrentRequests` | `number` | Scaling threshold — a new replica is added when concurrent HTTP requests exceed this value. |

Example `Pulumi.demo.yaml` excerpt:

```yaml
config:
  enablePrivateNetworking: false
  minReplicas: 0
  maxReplicas: 2
  concurrentRequests: 10
```

## Azure Resources

The following resources are provisioned in every environment:

| Resource | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Resource Group | `azure-native:resources:ResourceGroup` | Container for all environment resources |
| Log Analytics Workspace | `azure-native:operationalinsights:Workspace` | Backend storage for Container App logs |
| Application Insights | `azure-native:insights:Component` | Telemetry destination for processed spans |
| Container App Environment | `azure-native:app:ManagedEnvironment` | Hosting environment for the collector |
| Container App | `azure-native:app:ContainerApp` | Runs the OTel Collector image |

When `enablePrivateNetworking: true`, the following are also created:

| Resource | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Virtual Network | `azure-native:network:VirtualNetwork` | Private network for Container App Environment |
| Key Vault | `azure-native:keyvault:Vault` | Stores secrets for authenticated environments |
| Managed Identity | `azure-native:managedidentity:UserAssignedIdentity` | Grants the Container App access to Key Vault |

## Naming Convention

All resources follow the **Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF)** naming pattern:

```
<abbreviation>-<workload>-<environment>-<region>-<instance>
```

Examples:

| Resource | Name |
|---|---|
| Container App | `ca-<workload>-<environment>-<region>-001` |
| Container App Environment | `cae-<workload>-<environment>-<region>-001` |
| Application Insights | `appi-<workload>-<environment>-<region>-001` |
| Log Analytics Workspace | `log-<workload>-<environment>-<region>-001` |

## Collector Image

The gateway uses the official OpenTelemetry Collector Contrib image:

```
otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib:0.102.0
```

The collector is configured with:

- **Receiver**: `otlp` (HTTP on port 4318)
- **Exporter**: `azuremonitor` (forwards to Application Insights using the instrumentation key)
- **Pipeline**: `traces` — receiver → batch processor → exporter

## Pulumi State

Pulumi state is stored remotely. Ensure you are authenticated with the correct Pulumi backend before running `pulumi up`. Contact the platform team for access to the state storage account.
